en.extract:
The yellow wattlebird (Anthochaera paradoxa) is a species of bird in the honeyeater family Meliphagidae. 


Taxonomy
The yellow wattlebird was described by French zoologist François Marie Daudin as Corvus paradoxus in 1800. 
The generic name Anthochaera derives from the Ancient Greek anthos 'flower, bloom' and khairō 'enjoy'. The specific epithet paradoxa derives from the Ancient Greek paradoxos meaning 'strange, extraordinary'.
"Yellow wattlebird" has been designated as the official common name for the species by the International Ornithologists' Union (IOC). Other common names include the "long wattlebird" or "Tasmanian wattlebird".


Description
The yellow wattlebird is the largest of the honeyeaters, and is endemic to Tasmania. They are usually 37.5–45 centimetres (14.8–17.7 in) long. Body mass in males averages 168 g (5.9 oz) and in females averages 123 g (4.3 oz), with the largest males weighing up to 260 g (9.2 oz). They are named for the wattles hanging from the cheeks. Yellow wattlebirds are slim birds with a short, strong bill. They have a white face and black-streaked crown. They also have a long, pendulous yellow-orange wattle. The wattle becomes brighter during the breeding season. They have dark wings and a yellow belly, whereas the upperparts are grey to dusky brown. The female yellow wattlebird is much smaller than the male. The young yellow wattlebirds have much smaller wattles, a paler head, and a browner underbelly than the adult birds. 
The yellow wattlebird is similar in appearance to the little wattlebird and the red wattlebird.


Distribution and habitat
Yellow wattlebirds are common in Tasmania, especially in the eastern and central areas. They are uncommon on King Island, and two possible sightings recorded on the southern Mornington Peninsula in Victoria lack material evidence.
Yellow wattlebirds live in a variety of habitats including both dry and wet forests, and from sea level to the subalpine zone. They live in coastal heaths, forests and gardens near Eucalyptus trees. They also can be found in mountain shrubberies and open woodlands, particularly those dominated by Banksia. They have also been known to occur on golf courses, and in orchards, parks and gardens.


Behaviour
Yellow wattlebirds are active and acrobatic with a strong flight. They are fairly tame birds and often enter gardens looking for food.
Harsh, raucous and grating, their calls have often been compared to a person coughing or belching, with a gurgling growk or repeated clok sound
Yellow wattlebirds feed on the nectar of eucalypts and banksias, fruit, insects, spiders, honeydew, honey bees on the flight and manna (crystallised plant sap). They forage at all levels from the ground to the canopy. However, the blossoming of eucalyptus trees can be highly irregular in time and place, causing considerable changes from year to year in the breeding distribution of yellow wattlebirds, which rely on their nectar as a main source of food. Therefore, the most likely threat to the yellow wattlebird is unusual climatic conditions that can reduce food availability suddenly. Yellow wattlebirds can pollinate eucalyptus trees by carrying pollen in their bills or on the feathers of their heads.


Breeding
Yellow wattlebirds nest in breeding pairs and aggressively defend their territories from other birds. The nest of the yellow wattlebird is made by the female alone, and is a large, open saucer-shaped structure made of twigs and bark that are bound by wool. The inside of the nest is lined with wool and grass. The nests can be up to 13 centimetres (5.1 in) high and are found in trees or shrubs. Yellow wattlebirds lay 2–3 eggs that are salmon-red, spotted and blotched red-brown, purplish-red and blue-grey. Both the males and females incubate the egg and feed the young.


References


External links and further reading

Recordings of yellow wattlebird from Cornell Laboratory of Ornithology's Macaulay Library
Recordings of yellow wattlebird Archived 2022-03-01 at the Wayback Machine from Xeno-canto sound archive
Images of yellow wattlebird Archived 2020-06-30 at the Wayback Machine from Graeme Chapman's photo library

The '''yellow wattlebird''' ('''''Anthochaera paradoxa''''') is a species of [[bird]] in the honeyeater family [[Meliphagidae]].<ref name="iucn status 11 November 2021" /> 

==Taxonomy==
The yellow wattlebird was [[Species description|described]] by French zoologist [[François Marie Daudin]] as ''Corvus paradoxus'' in 1800. 

The generic name ''Anthochaera'' derives from the Ancient Greek ''anthos'' 'flower, bloom' and ''khairō'' 'enjoy'.<ref name=Helm>{{cite web|url=https://archive.org/details/Helm_Dictionary_of_Scientific_Bird_Names_by_James_A._Jobling|title=Helm Dictionary of Scientific Bird-names|last=Jobling|first=James A.|year=2010|access-date = 2020-04-23}}</ref> The [[specific epithet]] ''paradoxa'' derives from the Ancient Greek ''paradoxos'' meaning 'strange, extraordinary'.<ref name=Helm></ref>

"Yellow wattlebird" has been designated as the official common name for the species by the [[International Ornithologists' Union]] (IOC).<ref name="ioclist">{{cite web |editor1-last=Gill |editor1-first=Frank |editor1-link=Frank Gill (ornithologist) |editor2-last=Donsker |editor2-first=David |year=2025 |title=Honeyeaters |work=World Bird List Version 15.1 |url=https://www.worldbirdnames.org/new/bow/honeyeaters/ |publisher=International Ornithologists' Union |access-date=22 July 2025 |archive-date=7 April 2022 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220407033026/https://www.worldbirdnames.org/new/bow/honeyeaters/ |url-status=live }}</ref> Other common names include the "long wattlebird" or "Tasmanian wattlebird".<ref name="Pizzey">{{cite book |author=Graham Pizzey |author-link=Graham Pizzey |year=1980 |title=[[A Field Guide to the Birds of Australia (Pizzey)|A Field Guide to the Birds of Australia]] |location=Australia |publisher=[[Angus and Robertson]] |isbn=978-0-691-08483-1}}</ref>

==Description==
The yellow wattlebird is the largest of the honeyeaters,<ref name="Simpson">{{cite book |author1=Ken Simpson  |author2=Nicolas Day  |name-list-style=amp |year=2004 |title=[[Field Guide to the Birds of Australia (Simpson & Day)|Field Guide to the Birds of Australia]] |edition=7th |location=Princeton, NJ |publisher=[[Princeton University Press]] |isbn=978-0-691-12049-2}}</ref> and is [[endemic]] to [[Tasmania]]. They are usually {{convert|37.5|-|45|cm|in}} long.<ref name="Pizzey"/> Body mass in males averages {{convert|168|g|oz|abbr=on}} and in females averages {{convert|123|g|oz|abbr=on}}, with the largest males weighing up to {{convert|260|g|oz|abbr=on}}.<ref>{{cite book |title=CRC Handbook of Avian Body Masses |edition=2nd |editor-first=John B. Jr. |editor-last=Dunning |publisher=CRC Press |year=2008 |isbn=978-1-4200-6444-5}}</ref><ref>Higgins, P. J., L. Christidis, and H. Ford (2020). Yellow Wattlebird (Anthochaera paradoxa), version 1.0. In Birds of the World (J. del Hoyo, A. Elliott, J. Sargatal, D. A. Christie, and E. de Juana, Editors). Cornell Lab of Ornithology, Ithaca, NY, USA.</ref> They are named for the [[Wattle (anatomy)|wattles]] hanging from the cheeks.<ref name=MM>Morcombe, Michael (2012) Field guide to Australian birds. Pascal Press, Glebe, NSW. Revised edition. {{ISBN|978174021417-9}}</ref> Yellow wattlebirds are slim birds with a short, strong bill.<ref name="Backyard"/> They have a white face and black-streaked crown.<ref name="Simpson"/> They also have a long, pendulous yellow-orange wattle.<ref name="Simpson"/> The wattle becomes brighter during the breeding season.<ref name="Backyard"/> They have dark wings and a yellow belly,<ref name="Simpson"/> whereas the upperparts are grey to dusky brown.<ref name="Pizzey"/> The female yellow wattlebird is much smaller than the male.<ref name="Simpson"/> The young yellow wattlebirds have much smaller wattles, a paler head, and a browner underbelly than the adult birds.<ref name="Backyard"/> 

The yellow wattlebird is similar in appearance to the [[little wattlebird]] and the [[red wattlebird]].

==Distribution and habitat==
Yellow wattlebirds are common in Tasmania, especially in the eastern and central areas.<ref name="Backyard">{{cite web |url=http://birdsinbackyards.net/finder/display.cfm?id=288 |title=Yellow Wattlebird |publisher=Birds in Backyards |date=11 April 2006 |access-date=28 April 2008 |archive-date=6 May 2008 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20080506114949/http://www.birdsinbackyards.net/finder/display.cfm?id=288 |url-status=live }}</ref> They are uncommon on [[King Island (Tasmania)|King Island]], and two possible sightings recorded on the southern [[Mornington Peninsula]] in [[Victoria (Australia)|Victoria]] lack material evidence.<ref name="Pizzey"/>

Yellow wattlebirds live in a variety of habitats including both dry and wet forests, and from sea level to the [[subalpine zone]].<ref name="Backyard"/> They live in coastal [[heath]]s, forests and gardens near ''[[Eucalyptus]]'' trees.<ref name="Simpson"/> They also can be found in mountain shrubberies and open woodlands, particularly those dominated by ''[[Banksia]]''.<ref name="Pizzey"/> They have also been known to occur on [[golf course]]s, and in [[orchard]]s, parks and gardens.<ref name="Pizzey"/>

==Behaviour==
Yellow wattlebirds are active and acrobatic with a strong flight.<ref name="Pizzey"/> They are fairly tame birds and often enter gardens looking for food.<ref name="Pizzey"/>

Harsh, raucous and grating, their calls have often been compared to a person [[coughing]] or [[belching]],<ref name="Pizzey"/> with a gurgling ''growk'' or repeated ''clok'' sound<ref name=MM></ref>

Yellow wattlebirds feed on the [[nectar]] of eucalypts and banksias, fruit, [[insect]]s, [[spider]]s, [[Honeydew (secretion)|honeydew]], honey bees on the flight and manna (crystallised plant sap).<ref name="Action">{{cite web |url=http://www.environment.gov.au/biodiversity/threatened/publications/action/birds2000/pubs/yellow-wattlebird.pdf |title=The Action Plan for Australian Birds 2000: Recovery Outline – Yellow Wattlebird (King Island) |publisher=[[Department of the Environment, Water, Heritage and the Arts]] |date=27 April 2008 |access-date=28 April 2008 |archive-date=12 November 2011 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20111112162952/http://environment.gov.au/biodiversity/threatened/publications/action/birds2000/pubs/yellow-wattlebird.pdf |url-status=live }}</ref> They forage at all levels from the ground to the canopy.<ref name="Backyard"/> However, the blossoming of eucalyptus trees can be highly irregular in time and place, causing considerable changes from year to year in the breeding distribution of yellow wattlebirds, which rely on their nectar as a main source of food.<ref>{{cite journal |author1=M. G. Ridpath  |author2=R. E. Moreau  |name-list-style=amp |year=1966 |title=The birds of Tasmania: ecology and evolution |journal=[[Ibis (journal)|Ibis]] |volume=108 |issue=3 |pages=348–393 |doi=10.1111/j.1474-919X.1966.tb07349.x}}</ref> Therefore, the most likely threat to the yellow wattlebird is unusual climatic conditions that can reduce food availability suddenly.<ref name="Action"/> Yellow wattlebirds can pollinate eucalyptus trees by carrying pollen in their bills or on the feathers of their heads.<ref>{{cite journal |author=Andrew B. Hingston |author2=Brett D. Gartell |author3=Gina Pinchbeck |name-list-style=amp |year=2004 |title=How specialized is the plant–pollinator association between ''Eucalyptus globulus'' ssp. ''globulus'' and the swift parrot ''Lathamus discolor''? |journal=[[Austral Ecology]] |volume=29 |issue=6 |pages=624–630 |doi=10.1111/j.1442-9993.2004.01397.x |bibcode=2004AusEc..29..624H }}</ref>

===Breeding===
Yellow wattlebirds nest in breeding pairs and aggressively defend their territories from other birds.<ref name="Backyard"/> The nest of the yellow wattlebird is made by the female alone,<ref name="Backyard"/> and is a large, open saucer-shaped structure made of twigs and bark that are bound by wool.<ref name="Pizzey"/> The inside of the nest is lined with wool and grass.<ref name="Pizzey"/> The nests can be up to {{convert|13|cm}} high and are found in trees or shrubs.<ref name="Pizzey"/> Yellow wattlebirds lay 2–3 eggs that are salmon-red, spotted and blotched red-brown, purplish-red and blue-grey.<ref name="Pizzey"/> Both the males and females incubate the egg and feed the young.<ref name="Backyard"/>
